ref_notes: The very first US half cent — and only struck for one year with Liberty facing left. The obverse was quickly redesigned because the head-left orientation was unusual. Only 35,334 struck; this is a genuinely rare coin with only a few hundred surviving in collectible condition.

Head now faces right — the more conventional direction. The half cent was the smallest denomination ever struck by the US Mint; at the time, it had real purchasing power (a working man earned about $0.50/day).
Half cent 1795; the half cent was the smallest US denomination. Most circulated heavily in an era when a cent bought real goods. Problem-free examples are surprisingly scarce.

The Draped Bust half cent is an attractive early type. Robert Scot's Liberty portrait was used across the dime, quarter, half dollar, and dollar simultaneously — a unified look for early US coinage.

The 1855 half cent is among the final issues of the denomination. When it was abolished in 1857, the half cent had been so little used that most survivors are in high grade.

Last year of the half cent — abolished by the Mint Act of February 21, 1857, the same law that ended the large cent and introduced the small Flying Eagle cent. The half cent had a 64-year run (1793–1857). With inflation, it had become so worthless that even the poorest Americans barely used it.

The very first US cent — and it was controversial from day one. Citizens complained that the chain on the reverse symbolized slavery or bondage rather than unity. The design was scrapped after only ~36,000 were made. One of the most historically significant coins in American numismatics.

A hasty redesign after the Chain cent outrage — replaced within the same year. The wreath reverse and flowing hair Liberty were meant to be more appealing. Still a one-year type and highly sought by collectors.

A third obverse design in 1793 alone — the Mint was scrambling. The Liberty Cap (a symbol of freedom dating to ancient Rome) was added atop Liberty's pole. Only ~11,000 struck; the rarest of the 1793 cents.

Henry Voigt refined the dies; the head faces right for the first time. The 1794 cent comes in dozens of documented die varieties — early US cent collecting (called 'Early American Coppers') is its own deep hobby.

The 1799 cent is one of the most famous rarities in the entire large cent series. Most were likely melted or lost. In circulated grades it regularly sells for $1,000+; in Mint State it's a five-figure coin.
Another classic rarity — the 1804 cent, like the 1804 dollar, is a legendary date. With only ~756,000 struck and most heavily worn, problem-free examples are scarce.
John Reich redesigned Liberty with a more classical Roman look. The Classic Head cents are often overlooked by collectors, making them a relatively affordable early type.

The 1856 Flying Eagle cent is one of the most famous pattern coins ever made — Congress hadn't yet approved the small cent, so the ~600–1,000 pieces struck were distributed to congressmen as lobbying samples. Today it's collected as a regular issue and sells for thousands.

First year of Indian Head cent — designed by James B. Longacre. The 'Indian' is actually a figure of Liberty wearing a Native American headdress; legend says Longacre's daughter served as the model. Laurel wreath reverse used only this one year.
Indian Head cent 1860; designed by James B. Longacre. These were America's penny for 50 years (1859–1909) and remain widely collected today.

The most famous penny in American history. Victor David Brenner designed Lincoln's portrait for the centennial of his birth — the first time a real person appeared on a circulating US coin. The VDB initials on the reverse caused public outrage ('advertising on public money') and were removed after only ~484,000 were struck at San Francisco, creating the iconic 1909-S VDB key date.
First year Lincoln cent — Philadelphia VDB (27.9M struck) is common; San Francisco VDB (484,000) is the holy grail. Even the plain 1909-S (no VDB) is a semi-key at 1.8M mintage.
Indian Head cent 1909; designed by James B. Longacre. These were America's penny for 50 years (1859–1909) and remain widely collected today.

The most famous penny in American history. Victor David Brenner designed Lincoln's portrait for the centennial of his birth — the first time a real person appeared on a circulating US coin. The VDB initials on the reverse caused public outrage ('advertising on public money') and were removed after only ~484,000 were struck at San Francisco, creating the iconic 1909-S VDB key date.
The 1914-D is one of the most counterfeited coins in American numismatics — unscrupulous dealers have removed mint marks from 1944-D cents to fake it. Only 1.19M struck at Denver.
The 1914-D is one of the most counterfeited coins in American numismatics — unscrupulous dealers have removed mint marks from 1944-D cents to fake it. Only 1.19M struck at Denver.
The 1914-D is one of the most counterfeited coins in American numismatics — unscrupulous dealers have removed mint marks from 1944-D cents to fake it. Only 1.19M struck at Denver.

The 1922 'Plain' cent — Denver was the only mint striking cents this year, but some coins show a very weak or missing D mint mark due to worn dies. Three die pairs are known; the 'strong reverse' variety with no D is the most valuable and technically a mint error.

One of the most fascinating wartime stories in American coinage. Copper was needed for shell casings in WWII, so cents were made from zinc-coated steel — the only non-copper US cent in history. They rusted quickly, stuck to magnets, and were confused with dimes. The public hated them.
Denver Mint 1943 copper error — only one confirmed example exists, graded MS64BN by PCGS. Sold for $1.7M in 2010.

One of the most fascinating wartime stories in American coinage. Copper was needed for shell casings in WWII, so cents were made from zinc-coated steel — the only non-copper US cent in history. They rusted quickly, stuck to magnets, and were confused with dimes. The public hated them.

The rarest and most valuable Lincoln cent. A small number of copper planchets from 1942 were accidentally left in the presses when production switched to steel. Fewer than 30 are known. One sold for $1.7 million in 2010. If you find a 1943 cent that doesn't stick to a magnet — you might be holding a fortune.

One of the most fascinating wartime stories in American coinage. Copper was needed for shell casings in WWII, so cents were made from zinc-coated steel — the only non-copper US cent in history. They rusted quickly, stuck to magnets, and were confused with dimes. The public hated them.
San Francisco 1943 copper error — fewer than 10 known. Equally valuable to the Philadelphia version.
The reverse of the 1943 error — in 1944, copper was restored, but some steel planchets from 1943 slipped through. Fewer than 30 known across all mints. Worth tens of thousands of dollars.

The most famous doubled die error in American numismatics. The doubling on LIBERTY, IN GOD WE TRUST, and the date is so dramatic it's visible to the naked eye. Legend says the error was discovered in cigarette vending machine change. Around 24,000 error coins entered circulation; today they sell for $1,000–$15,000 depending on grade.
Lincoln Memorial cent 1959; Frank Gasparro's Memorial reverse ran 1959–2008. Despite billions minted, condition rarities exist in MS65 Red and above.

Four reverses for Lincoln's 200th birthday — Birth & Early Childhood (log cabin), Formative Years (rail splitter), Professional Life (Illinois statehouse), Presidency (half-finished Capitol dome). The log cabin design in copper (for collectors) has a .05% tin/zinc alloy matching the original bronze formula.

The two-cent piece holds a unique place in American history — it was the FIRST US coin to bear the motto 'IN GOD WE TRUST,' added at the suggestion of Rev. Mark Watkinson during the Civil War. The motto was later added to other denominations and eventually became mandatory on all US coins by act of Congress in 1955.

The 1872 two-cent piece is the key date of the short series — only 65,000 struck for circulation. The denomination was abolished in 1873 after just 9 years; the public found the two-cent piece redundant.

The first three-cent coin in US history — nicknamed the 'trime.' It was authorized partly to facilitate the purchase of 3-cent stamps. At 75% silver, it was the lowest silver content of any US coin to that point. At just 14mm, it was and remains the smallest US silver coin ever made.

The three-cent nickel was introduced to replace the silver trime, which had been hoarded. It's also notable as the first US coin to not contain any precious metal — 75% copper / 25% nickel. The design by James B. Longacre features a simple Liberty head with no stars.

The very first US nickel five-cent piece — struck in 75% copper / 25% nickel, a composition still used today. The rays between the stars on the reverse were removed after one year because they caused striking problems. Designed by James B. Longacre.
Shield nickel 1867; the first US nickel 5-cent piece. Many dates are underrated — the short series (1866–1883) is completable with patience.
Rays removed to improve strike quality. The Shield nickel design is bold and patriotic but was never beloved — it's a distinctly Civil War-era coin.

First issue of 1883 — no word CENTS below the V. Gold-plated versions were passed as $5 gold coins, particularly by a deaf-mute woman named Josh Tatum who never technically said they were $5. Congress demanded the fix.
Second issue of 1883 — CENTS added below the V after the gold-plating scandal. Over 16 million struck to replace the No CENTS coins already in circulation.

First year of one of America's most beloved coin designs. James Earle Fraser designed both sides from real subjects: the obverse composite portrait drawn from three Native American chiefs (Iron Tail, Two Moons, and John Big Tree), and the reverse from Black Diamond, a bison at the Central Park Zoo. The raised mound the buffalo stands on wore down quickly.

Mid-year change — the raised mound was flattened to a flat ground to prevent the date from wearing off quickly. Both Type I and Type II were struck in 1913, making it the only year with both varieties.

The most famous Buffalo nickel variety and one of the most famous US coin errors. A mint worker over-polished a die to remove a gouge, accidentally grinding away the buffalo's front right leg. The result looks comically wrong — and is worth $500+ even in low grades. Always examine 1937-D nickels carefully.

First year of Jefferson nickel — designed by Felix Schlag, who won a competition of 390 artists. The winning design placed Monticello (Jefferson's Virginia home) on the reverse — it remains there to this day, making the Jefferson nickel one of the longest-running unchanged designs in US history.
Jefferson nickel 1938; Full Steps (FS) designation adds significant premium for coins with complete Monticello steps — check the bottom two steps for completeness.

Nickel was critical for WWII armor plating, so the composition was changed to 35% silver / 56% copper / 9% manganese. The large P, D, or S mint mark on the reverse (above Monticello) identifies these 'war nickels.' The P mark was the first time Philadelphia ever put its mint mark on a circulating coin.

The 1950-D is the key date of the Jefferson nickel series — only 2,630,030 struck at Denver. Speculators hoarded them heavily at the time, which is why so many survive in high grades today (the opposite of most key dates). Nevertheless a genuine rarity with face value still under a nickel.

The Westward Journey nickels commemorated the Lewis & Clark bicentennial. Two reverses in 2004 — the Keelboat (used by Lewis & Clark) and the American Bison (Pacific Ocean view in 2005). The obverse was also redesigned — the first change to Jefferson's portrait in 66 years.

The Westward Journey nickels commemorated the Lewis & Clark bicentennial. Two reverses in 2004 — the Keelboat (used by Lewis & Clark) and the American Bison (Pacific Ocean view in 2005). The obverse was also redesigned — the first change to Jefferson's portrait in 66 years.

The Westward Journey nickels commemorated the Lewis & Clark bicentennial. Two reverses in 2004 — the Keelboat (used by Lewis & Clark) and the American Bison (Pacific Ocean view in 2005). The obverse was also redesigned — the first change to Jefferson's portrait in 66 years.

Monticello returned to the reverse in 2006 with a fresh new portrait of Jefferson on the obverse — the first full-face portrait on any circulating US coin in the modern era. Felix Schlag's original Monticello design was refined.

The 1894-S Barber dime is one of the most legendary coins in American numismatics. Only 24 were struck — apparently as presentation pieces for San Francisco Fed bankers. Only 9 are known today. One sold for $1.99 million in 2007. A Mint engraver reportedly gave 3 to his daughter, who spent one on ice cream — a story every coin collector knows.

The twenty-cent piece is one of the shortest-lived and most confusing denominations in US history. It was so similar in size, design, and value to the quarter dollar that the public couldn't tell them apart. Despite being legal tender, merchants and citizens refused them. Production effectively ceased after 1876.

The twenty-cent piece is one of the shortest-lived and most confusing denominations in US history. It was so similar in size, design, and value to the quarter dollar that the public couldn't tell them apart. Despite being legal tender, merchants and citizens refused them. Production effectively ceased after 1876.

The 1876-CC twenty-cent piece — only about 1,155 are believed to survive from the original 10,000 struck. Most were melted. In any grade these are major rarities. The denomination's failure was so complete that Congress abolished it in 1878 — a rare admission by Congress that a coin design had completely failed.

Arrows and rays variety — the rays between the stars were only used this one year, making 1853 a one-year type. Silver content was reduced and the Mint added arrows and rays to signal the change. The rays caused striking difficulties and were removed in 1854.

First American Women quarter — Maya Angelou was chosen to launch the program. The AWQ program runs 2022–2025 honoring notable American women, five per year. It's the first time multiple women have been featured on US circulating quarters.
American Women quarter — 2022–2025 program; five designs per year honoring notable American women. First circulating US coin program dedicated entirely to women.

Kennedy half dollar 1776; clad composition since 1971. Half dollars largely disappeared from circulation by the late 1970s — vending machines and parking meters didn't accept them, so billions were saved in bank vaults.

The first US half dollar — struck in the same year as the first US dollar. Only ~24,000 survive across all grades. The flowing hair Liberty design was the work of Robert Scot, the first Chief Engraver of the US Mint.
Extremely rare; 15 stars and 16 stars varieties

Reeded edge introduced mid-year

First year of the Franklin half dollar — Benjamin Franklin replaced Walking Liberty at the insistence of Mint Director Nellie Tayloe Ross. Many collectors were unhappy with the change. The reverse features the Liberty Bell with a small eagle (required by law for half dollars). Full Bell Lines (FBL) is the key grade designation.

The Trade dollar was specifically designed for commerce with China and Asia — it weighed more than the standard dollar (420 grains vs. 412.5 grains) to compete with the Mexican peso that dominated Pacific trade. 'TRADE DOLLAR' and its weight are prominently stamped on the reverse. It worked commercially but caused chaos domestically when Congress made it legal tender — merchants were stuck with debased coins.

The 1885 Trade Dollar — proof only, only 5 known to exist. One of the rarest and most valuable of all US coins. The denomination had lost its legal tender status in 1876 and most remaining coins were being used only in Asia. The 1884 and 1885 'proofs' may have been struck as numismatic items — their legitimacy as regular issues has been debated for over a century.

The Gobrecht dollar is one of the most romantically beautiful US coins — designed by Christian Gobrecht, the obverse shows a seated Liberty (which became the template for all Seated Liberty coinage). The reverse has a soaring eagle in a field of stars. Most were struck as patterns or presentation pieces; determining which are 'originals' vs 'restrikes' has occupied numismatists for over a century.

The 1870-S Seated Liberty dollar is one of the great numismatic mysteries — only 9 are known, and it's believed one was placed in the cornerstone of the new San Francisco Mint building. Were any others saved? Probably few were meant to circulate.

First year of the Morgan dollar — designed by George T. Morgan, a British-born engraver hired specifically for this project. The obverse model was Philadelphia schoolteacher Anna Willess Williams. Eight tail feathers, seven tail feathers, and seven over eight tail feather varieties all exist in 1878 — a three-way variety hunt in one year.

Morgan dollars resumed after a 17-year gap (1904–1921). The Peace dollar replaced them after 1921. These final Morgans have a slightly different look — the dies were made from scratch rather than from original hubs.

First year of the Peace dollar — designed by Anthony de Francisci, whose wife Teresa served as the model for Liberty. The high relief design of 1921 is stunning but different from subsequent years. Only one year of high relief — 1922 onward used lower relief for easier striking.

First year of the Eisenhower dollar — designed by Frank Gasparro. Honored both President Eisenhower and the Apollo 11 moon landing (a small Eagle and earth on the reverse). The coin was large and heavy and largely rejected by the public, setting the stage for the smaller SBA dollar.

Narrow Rim. Type 1 — the common variety, smaller rim, date further from rim. First circulating US coin to honor a real woman. The SBA dollar was immediately unpopular — too similar in size to a quarter, causing widespread confusion. The 'Near Date' and 'Far Date' varieties exist for 1979; the Wide Rim (Near Date) variety is scarcer.

The 'Near Date' and 'Far Date' varieties exist for 1979; the Wide Rim (Near Date) variety is scarcer.

After the disastrous public reception, SBA dollars were halted after 1981... then revived for one more year in 1999 when Sacagawea dollar dies weren't ready yet. The 1981-S Type II clear S proof is a variety.

The SBA returned unexpectedly in 1999 — a stopgap while Sacagawea dollar production ramped up. Many were used in mass transit fare machines. The 'Close AM' and 'Open AM' die varieties continue into this final year.

The 2000-W Sacagawea/State Quarter mule — a coin accidentally struck with a Sacagawea obverse and a New Hampshire state quarter reverse. Only 19 are known. One sold for $280,000. This is the only 'mule' error in modern US mint history.

First Presidential dollar — George Washington leads the series. The motto 'In God We Trust' and 'E Pluribus Unum' were moved to the edge of the coin, which caused controversy and some coins were struck without any edge lettering (the 'Godless dollars') — worth $50–$200 each.
Second Presidential dollar; edge lettering varieties continue. The program was intended to release four presidents per year until all deceased presidents were honored.
Presidential dollar — released four per year 2007–2016. Edge lettering includes date, mint mark, and mottoes. Varieties include missing edge lettering ('Godless dollars') on 2007 issues.

The first US commemorative coin — struck for the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago celebrating the 400th anniversary of Columbus's arrival. Over 1 million were sold at $1 each (double face value). This coin launched the entire commemorative coin concept in America.

The only commemorative US quarter ever struck in the classic era (1892–1954). Commemorates the 400th anniversary of Columbus; Queen Isabella of Spain appears — the first woman on a US coin. Only 24,214 struck.
Classic commemorative half dollar; Columbian Exposition
Classic commemorative half dollar; Isabella Quarter (25c)
Classic commemorative half dollar; Lafayette Dollar (S$1)

First US commemorative silver dollar — and the first US coin to depict a real person (Lafayette, Washington). Sold at the Paris Exposition; the proceeds funded a Lafayette statue. A landmark in US numismatic history.

The largest US coin ever struck for public sale — a $50 gold piece weighing 83.59 grams. Only 645 of the octagonal version were sold (the round version only 483). These are among the rarest and most valuable of all US coins, regularly selling for $100,000+.

The first modern US commemorative coin — launched what became the most prolific commemorative program in US history. The Washington half dollar was struck in 90% silver, the first 90% silver coin since 1964. Designed by Elizabeth Jones.

Curved coin

The first curved US coin — a revolutionary design concept. The concave obverse shows a baseball glove; the convex reverse shows a baseball. Demand was enormous; mintage limits were hit in hours. A genuinely new chapter in US coin design.

First Canadian cent — struck in London by the Royal Mint for the Province of Canada, not yet a dominion. Two varieties: 'Narrow 9' and 'Wide 9' in the date. The first coin ever struck for Canadian use as a united nation. Key date.
Proof or Specimen set issue 1858; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets

1859 has a famous variety: the Double Punched 9 (9/8 overdate) where a previous 8 was re-engraved as 9. The regular 1859 is one of the most common Victorian large cents. The DP9 variety is scarce and valuable.
Proof or Specimen set issue 1876; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets
H mint mark = Heaton Mint, Birmingham, England. Many Victorian Canadian cents were struck in England before the Ottawa Mint opened.
Proof or Specimen set issue 1881; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets
Proof or Specimen set issue 1882; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets
The 1884 large cent has two obverse varieties — the blunt 4 and the pointed 4 in the date. A popular variety hunt.
Three varieties: Large Date Large Leaves, Large Date Small Leaves, Small Date Small Leaves — among the most collected varieties in Canadian numismatics.
Lower mintage; semi-key date
Last year with the portrait of Queen Victoria, who died in January 1901. Her 64-year reign saw Canadian coinage evolve from colonial to national.
First year Edward VII large cent — new portrait by G.W. de Saulles. A new king, a new obverse.
Semi-key date
Semi-key date — low mintage for Edward VII era
Heaton Mint Birmingham issue — the H mintmark is found on the reverse near the date
Proof or Specimen set issue 1908; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets
Last year Edward VII — the King died in May 1910. A new portrait would come with George V.
First year George V large cent. Two varieties: 'Leaves' and 'Victorian Leaves' — the Victorian Leaves variety uses older dies.
WWI production boom
Last year of the Canadian large cent — replaced by the small cent in 1920. The large cent ran for 62 years. Like the US large cent, it simply became too large and costly to produce.
First year Canadian small cent — same year as last large cent. Maple leaves replaced the traditional reverse. 95.5% copper.
The 1922 small cent is a major key date — only 1.24M struck. Far scarcer than most collectors expect.
Key date — even lower mintage than 1922. The 1923 is the rarest George V small cent in mint state.
Semi-key date
One of the key dates of the small cent series — under 1.1M struck.
Semi-key date

The 1936 'Dot' cent — struck in 1937 with a 1936 die but with a tiny dot below the date to indicate it was a later striking. Only 3 confirmed examples known. One sold for $400,000 CAD. The rarest Canadian coin ever offered at public auction.
Proof or Specimen set issue 1936; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets

First year George VI cent — the obverse inscription changes to GEORGIVS VI. Varieties include near 7 and far 7 in the date. Also the year Canada adopted the Maple Leaf design by Emanuel Hahn that would last until decimalization.
WWII begins; Canadian production ramps up.
High wartime mintage
Tombac (bronze-colored steel) used 1942-43 to conserve copper for war. The 12-sided 'Victory' design was used on Canadian small cents 1943-1952, making them easy to identify by touch.

1947 has the famous 'Maple Leaf' variety — a small maple leaf after the date indicates the coin was struck in 1948 with 1947 dies (waiting for a new effigy removing 'ET IND IMP' after Indian independence). This variety commands a premium.

coin_type: Small Cent - George VI - Maple Leaf Variety
ref_notes: 1947 Maple Leaf — a small maple leaf after the date signifies these were struck in 1948 with 1947 dies, pending a new portrait removing 'ET IND IMP' following Indian independence. Scarcer than the regular 1947 and commands a premium.
New obverse — 'ET IND IMP' (Emperor of India) removed from the legend after Indian independence in 1947.
Last year George VI; the King died February 6, 1952. Coins struck before and after his death both bear the 1952 date.
First year Elizabeth II cent — two major varieties: 'No Shoulder Fold' (NSF) and 'Shoulder Fold' (SF). The NSF is scarcer and more valuable. A new queen, a new obverse portrait by Mary Gillick.

coin_type: Small Cent - Elizabeth II - No Shoulder Fold
ref_notes: The 1953 NSF (No Shoulder Fold) variety — the fold in Queen Elizabeth's gown strap was absent on early dies. Scarcer than the SF variety and worth more. An important first-year variety.

coin_type: Small Cent - Elizabeth II - Shoulder Fold
ref_notes: The 1953 SF (Shoulder Fold) is the more common of the two 1953 varieties.
Proof or Specimen set issue 1953; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets
The 1954 'No Shoulder Fold' variety is one of the key dates of the Elizabeth II small cent series.
Proof or Specimen set issue 1955; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets

Proof or Specimen set issue 1961; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets
Proof or Specimen set issue 1963; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets

Proof or Specimen set issue 1965; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets
Confederation Centennial — special reverse for Canada's 100th birthday showing a dove in flight, designed by Alex Colville. The centennial coins are widely collected as a set.

coin_type: Small Cent - Elizabeth II - Centennial Dove
ref_notes: Canada's Confederation Centennial — Alex Colville's dove in flight design replaced the maple twig for 1967 only. A beautiful one-year type and the most widely collected year in the Canadian cent series.
Proof or Specimen set issue 1967; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets

New portrait by Susanna Blunt — third portrait of Elizabeth II on Canadian coins. Also the Golden Jubilee year (50th anniversary of her reign).
Proof or Specimen set issue 2003; Royal Canadian Mint collector sets
Remembrance poppy cent — a colored red poppy on the reverse, first colored circulating coin in Canadian history.

coin_type: Small Cent - Elizabeth II - Remembrance Poppy
ref_notes: The world's first colored circulating coin — a red poppy applied to the reverse. Issued for Remembrance Day. Some examples are found with the color worn off (common on circulated pieces).

FINAL YEAR of the Canadian cent — eliminated February 4, 2013 due to the cost of production exceeding face value (1.6 cents to make a 1-cent coin). Canadians were instructed to round cash transactions to the nearest 5 cents. The penny's elimination was controversial but quickly accepted. A piece of Canadian history ended quietly.
First Canadian $2 coin ever struck — the 'Toonie'. A bimetallic coin with aluminum bronze outer ring and nickel center. Polar bear design by Brent Townsend. Key first-year.
1996 Proof Toonie — Royal Canadian Mint prestige set issue.
Low mintage year — only 17M struck vs 375M in 1996.
Low mintage.
Last year of uncrowned effigy obverse on regular strikes.
New crowned effigy of Elizabeth II by Susanna Blunt introduced.
New security features: multi-ply plated steel center, modified edge lettering. Slightly lighter at 6.92g.
150th anniversary of Canadian Confederation — commemorative design available alongside regular polar bear reverse.
Special reverse: 'Canada 150' logo celebrating 150 years of Confederation. Widely circulated.
COVID-19 pandemic year — reduced mintage.
First year featuring King Charles III obverse by Steven Rosati.

2026 Semiquincentennial issue. The Lincoln cent, introduced in 1909, receives a special one-year reverse design honoring the 250th anniversary of American independence. Struck at Philadelphia, Denver, and San Francisco (Proof). The Lincoln Shield cent series has run continuously since 2010.

2026 Semiquincentennial issue. The Lincoln cent, introduced in 1909, receives a special one-year reverse design honoring the 250th anniversary of American independence. Struck at Philadelphia, Denver, and San Francisco (Proof). The Lincoln Shield cent series has run continuously since 2010.

2026 Semiquincentennial issue. The Lincoln cent, introduced in 1909, receives a special one-year reverse design honoring the 250th anniversary of American independence. Struck at Philadelphia, Denver, and San Francisco (Proof). The Lincoln Shield cent series has run continuously since 2010.

2026 Semiquincentennial issue. The Jefferson nickel, introduced in 1938, features a one-year reverse design commemorating the 250th anniversary of American independence. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver. The Jefferson nickel's obverse portrait by Felix Schlag has been updated several times (2005, 2006) but the Monticello reverse returned in 2006.

2026 Semiquincentennial issue. The Jefferson nickel, introduced in 1938, features a one-year reverse design commemorating the 250th anniversary of American independence. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver. The Jefferson nickel's obverse portrait by Felix Schlag has been updated several times (2005, 2006) but the Monticello reverse returned in 2006.

2026 Semiquincentennial issue. The Roosevelt dime, introduced in 1946 following FDR's death, receives a one-year reverse design for the 250th anniversary of American independence. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver. The Roosevelt dime has been struck in copper-nickel clad since 1965; the 90% silver composition ended in 1964.

2026 Semiquincentennial issue. The Roosevelt dime, introduced in 1946 following FDR's death, receives a one-year reverse design for the 250th anniversary of American independence. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver. The Roosevelt dime has been struck in copper-nickel clad since 1965; the 90% silver composition ended in 1964.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Mayflower Compact design. One of five quarter designs released in 2026 for the 250th anniversary of American independence. The Mayflower Compact (1620) was the first governing document of Plymouth Colony, signed by the Pilgrims aboard the Mayflower — an early example of self-government in what would become the United States. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Mayflower Compact design. One of five quarter designs released in 2026 for the 250th anniversary of American independence. The Mayflower Compact (1620) was the first governing document of Plymouth Colony, signed by the Pilgrims aboard the Mayflower — an early example of self-government in what would become the United States. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Revolutionary War design. One of five quarter designs for the 250th anniversary. The Revolutionary War (1775–1783) secured American independence from Great Britain. This design honors the soldiers and civilians who fought for liberty. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Revolutionary War design. One of five quarter designs for the 250th anniversary. The Revolutionary War (1775–1783) secured American independence from Great Britain. This design honors the soldiers and civilians who fought for liberty. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Declaration of Independence design. One of five quarter designs for the 250th anniversary. The Declaration of Independence, adopted July 4, 1776, proclaimed the thirteen colonies' separation from Britain and articulated the founding principles of liberty and equality. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Declaration of Independence design. One of five quarter designs for the 250th anniversary. The Declaration of Independence, adopted July 4, 1776, proclaimed the thirteen colonies' separation from Britain and articulated the founding principles of liberty and equality. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Constitution design. One of five quarter designs for the 250th anniversary. The U.S. Constitution, ratified in 1788 and effective 1789, established the framework of the federal government and remains the supreme law of the land. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Constitution design. One of five quarter designs for the 250th anniversary. The U.S. Constitution, ratified in 1788 and effective 1789, established the framework of the federal government and remains the supreme law of the land. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Gettysburg Address design. One of five quarter designs for the 250th anniversary. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (November 19, 1863) redefined the Civil War as a struggle for the principles of the Declaration — 'a new birth of freedom.' Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial Quarter — Gettysburg Address design. One of five quarter designs for the 250th anniversary. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (November 19, 1863) redefined the Civil War as a struggle for the principles of the Declaration — 'a new birth of freedom.' Struck at Philadelphia and Denver.

2026 Semiquincentennial issue. The Kennedy half dollar, introduced in 1964 after President Kennedy's assassination, receives a one-year reverse design for the 250th anniversary of American independence. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver. The half dollar has been copper-nickel clad since 1971 (40% silver 1965–1970, 90% silver 1964 only). Circulation strikes ended in 2001; modern issues are numismatic-only.

2026 Semiquincentennial issue. The Kennedy half dollar, introduced in 1964 after President Kennedy's assassination, receives a one-year reverse design for the 250th anniversary of American independence. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver. The half dollar has been copper-nickel clad since 1971 (40% silver 1965–1970, 90% silver 1964 only). Circulation strikes ended in 2001; modern issues are numismatic-only.

2026 Semiquincentennial Dollar. A one-year circulating commemorative dollar for the 250th anniversary of American independence. The design honors the founding ideals of liberty, equality, and self-government. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver in the standard 'golden dollar' manganese-brass clad composition (88.5% Cu, 6% Zn, 3.5% Mn, 2% Ni). The Native American and American Innovation dollar programs continue alongside this issue.

2026 Semiquincentennial Dollar. A one-year circulating commemorative dollar for the 250th anniversary of American independence. The design honors the founding ideals of liberty, equality, and self-government. Struck at Philadelphia and Denver in the standard 'golden dollar' manganese-brass clad composition (88.5% Cu, 6% Zn, 3.5% Mn, 2% Ni). The Native American and American Innovation dollar programs continue alongside this issue.
